title: The Dark Night of the Soul and Spiritual Bypassing
meta_description: You're here, aren't you? Probably scrolling on your phone at three in the morning, heart pounding, mind racing. You've felt it. That deep, unsettling ache,
body:
The Dark Night of the Soul and Spiritual Bypassing

You're here, aren't you? Probably scrolling on your phone at three in the morning, heart pounding, mind racing. You've felt it. That deep, unsettling ache, that sense of everything you thought you knew crumbling around you. They call it the Dark Night of the Soul. And let me tell you, it's not some poetic phrase for a bad week. It's a tearing, a stripping away, and it feels like you're losing your damn mind.

I've been there. I know that terror, that isolation. The feeling like you're the only one experiencing this raw, visceral breakdown. It's not just sadness; it's an existential crisis that shakes you to your core. You question everything: your purpose, your sanity, even the reality you once took for granted. It’s like someone ripped the floorboards out from under your life, and you’re free-falling in the dark. Right? You're looking for answers, for a lifeline, and the internet is a minefield of fluffy spiritual platitudes that just make you feel worse. They don't get it. They haven't been through the fire. I have. And I'm here to tell you, you're not alone, and you're not broken.

The Dark Night - it's a phrase that's been around for centuries. You can read about it in The Dark Night of the Soul by St. John of the Cross (paid link), a classic that details the spiritual purgation of the mystic. But you don't need to be a mystic to experience it. It's happening to regular people like you and me. It's a sign that something deep within you is stirring, waking up, and demanding attention. It's uncomfortable. It's terrifying. But it's also a necessary part of growth. It's not a punishment; it's a process. A brutal, relentless process, but a process nonetheless.

What the Hell is Happening to Me? The Dark Night Explained

So, what exactly is this "Dark Night"? Look, it's not a clinical diagnosis. You won't find it in the DSM-5. But it's a very real experience, often described as a period of intense spiritual desolation, confusion, and despair. It feels like God - or whatever higher power you believe in, or even just the universe - has abandoned you. Your old beliefs feel hollow. Your usual coping mechanisms don't work. The joy you once felt, even in small things, is gone. It's like a spiritual void has opened up inside you, swallowing everything.

You might find yourself questioning your faith, your relationships, your career, your very existence. There's a deep sense of meaninglessness. You might experience intense anxiety, depression, insomnia, and physical symptoms that doctors can't explain. I'm talking about heart palpitations, vibrating sensations, unexplained pains, extreme fatigue, or sudden bursts of energy that leave you drained. It's all part of the package, unfortunately. Your body and mind are reacting to a deep internal shift. It's not just "stress." It's deeper. It's a stripping away of the ego, of your false self, of everything that isn't truly you.

This isn't about being sad because your cat died or you lost your job. Those are tough, sure. But the Dark Night goes beyond circumstantial suffering. It's an internal dismantling. It's the universe saying, "Okay, we're done with your old programming. Time for an upgrade." And the upgrade process feels like a demolition. You're being forced to confront your shadows, your traumas, your deepest fears. All the stuff you've buried for years? It's bubbling up to the surface, demanding to be seen and processed. It's messy. It's ugly. But it's also where the real healing begins.

Kundalini Awakening: When the Fire Ignites

Sometimes, this Dark Night experience is intertwined with something even more intense: a kundalini awakening. Know what I mean? If you're feeling strange electrical sensations, intense heat, involuntary movements, or visions, you might be experiencing this. Kundalini is often described as a dormant spiritual energy at the base of your spine. When it "wakes up," it can travel through your energy channels, clearing blockages and bringing about deep changes. But it's not always blissful enlightenment, especially at first.

For many, myself included, it's a hellish ride. It can feel like your nervous system is on fire. You might have periods of intense bliss followed by equally intense terror. Your senses might become hyper-sensitive. You might feel disconnected from reality, like you're living in a dream. This is often called a "spiritual emergency." It's when the spiritual process gets so intense that it overwhelms your psychological and physical systems. When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) is a really good book if you're dealing with this. She gets it. She talks about the messiness, the confusion, the sheer overwhelming nature of it all. It's not all rainbows and unicorns, believe me.

You might feel like you're having a mental breakdown, and in some ways, you are - a breakdown of the old self. But it's not necessarily a mental illness. It's a spiritual crisis that can manifest with symptoms that look a lot like mental illness. That's why it's so important to find practitioners who understand spiritual emergence, not just traditional psychiatry. You need someone who can differentiate between a spiritual process and a purely psychological one, someone who won't just medicate you into oblivion without addressing the root cause. This is a big deal, because getting the wrong help can make things worse. Does that land?

The Trap of Spiritual Bypassing

Okay, so you're in the thick of it. You're feeling like absolute garbage. And then you go looking for answers, and what do you find? A ton of "spiritual" advice that tells you to "just be positive," "raise your vibration," "manifest joy," or "release your resistance." This is spiritual bypassing, and it's a dangerous trap. It's a way of using spiritual ideas and practices to avoid dealing with unresolved emotional wounds, psychological issues, and developmental tasks. It's like putting a pretty band-aid on a gaping wound and pretending it's fine. It's not fine. It's just hidden.

I see it everywhere. People telling you to "just meditate away your depression" or "love and light your anxiety into submission." It's bullshit, honestly. While meditation and positive thinking can be helpful tools, they become bypassing when they're used to suppress, deny, or rationalize away your pain instead of actually feeling and processing it. You can't "affirm" away deep-seated trauma. You can't "manifest" your way out of a spiritual emergency. You have to go through it.

Here's the thing: the Dark Night demands that you confront your pain, not pretend it doesn't exist. Spiritual bypassing tells you that your pain is "low vibration" or "not spiritual" and that you should just ignore it. This just shoves it back down, where it festers and causes more problems later. It creates a disconnect between your spiritual aspirations and your human reality. You end up feeling guilty for feeling bad, which just adds another layer of suffering. You're already in hell; you don't need more guilt on top of it.

How to Spot Bypassing (and avoid it yourself)

So, how do you know if you're doing it, or if someone is feeding you this crap? Here are some signs:

  • Excessive positivity: Constantly dismissing negative emotions or experiences as "bad" or "unspiritual."
  • Premature forgiveness: Forgiving someone before you've even processed the hurt they caused.
  • Avoidance of difficult emotions: Using spiritual practices like meditation or affirmations to escape feelings of anger, sadness, or fear.
  • Detachment from reality: Focusing solely on "higher planes" or "oneness" while ignoring real-world problems or responsibilities.
  • Blaming the victim: Telling someone their suffering is due to their "vibration" or "lack of faith" instead of acknowledging external factors or systemic issues.
  • Obsession with "good vibes": Creating a bubble where only "positive" thoughts and feelings are allowed, pushing away anything uncomfortable.

You're not supposed to be "happy" all the time, especially when you're going through something this intense. It's okay to be angry. It's okay to be sad. It's okay to be terrified. These emotions are messengers. They're telling you something needs attention. When you bypass them, you're silencing the message. And the message will just get louder, believe me. You can read more about what happens during these intense periods in Waking Up Is Hard to Do: Navigating Spiritual Emergence (paid link). It helps you understand that what you're going through is a recognized, albeit often misunderstood, process.

Integration: The Only Way Out is Through

So, if you can't bypass it, what do you do? You integrate. This is the hard part. This is where the real work happens. Integration means bringing all parts of yourself - the light and the shadow, the spiritual and the human, the broken and the whole - together. It means acknowledging your pain, feeling it, processing it, and learning from it. It's not about getting rid of the "bad" stuff; it's about making peace with it and understanding its role in your growth.

When you're in the Dark Night, it feels like you're shattering. Integration is about putting the pieces back together, but not in the same way they were before. It's like kintsugi, the Japanese art of repairing broken pottery with gold lacquer. The cracks are still there, but they're filled with something precious, making the object even more beautiful and unique. You won't be the same person after this. You'll be stronger, deeper, more compassionate, more authentic. But you have to let yourself be broken first.

This process takes time. It takes patience. It takes courage. And it takes a willingness to sit in the discomfort. There's no quick fix. There's no magic pill. Anyone who tells you otherwise is selling you snake oil. You need to create a safe container for yourself to go through this. That means:

  • Finding the right support: This might be a therapist who understands spiritual emergency, a spiritual director, a trusted friend, or a support group. Someone who won't judge you or try to "fix" you, but will simply hold space for your experience.
  • Grounding practices: When your energy is all over the place, you need to ground yourself. This could be spending time in nature, eating nourishing food, gentle movement, or sensory activities that bring you back into your body. Don't underestimate the power of simple things like walking barefoot on the grass or taking a warm bath.
  • Allowing yourself to feel: This is crucial. Don't fight the emotions. Let them come. Cry. Scream into a pillow. Write in a journal. Just let it out. Suppressing it only prolongs the suffering.
  • Rest: Your body and mind are working overtime. You need to rest, truly rest. Not just sleep, but periods of quiet, stillness, and non-doing.
  • Patience: This isn't a race. There's no timeline for spiritual awakening or integration. It unfolds at its own pace. Trust the process, even when it feels like it's never going to end.

It's okay to not be okay. It's okay to admit you're struggling. This isn't a sign of weakness; it's a sign of immense strength. You're confronting parts of yourself that most people spend their entire lives avoiding. That's brave as hell. The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link) is another excellent resource for understanding these intense experiences. They talk about spiritual crises, psychospiritual problems, and how to deal with them. It's not fluffy. It's real.

You're Not Crazy, You're Waking Up

I know it feels like you're going crazy. I know that feeling of utter bewilderment and loneliness. But here's the truth is: you're not crazy. You're waking up. And waking up is messy. It's uncomfortable. It's disorienting. It's everything they don't show you in those Instagram posts about "spiritual journeys." They show the mountaintop, but they don't show the brutal climb through the wilderness, the blisters, the falls, the moments you want to give up.

This Dark Night, this spiritual emergency, it's an initiation. It's stripping away everything that isn't authentic, everything that's holding you back from your true self. It's painful, yes. But on the other side of this pain is immense freedom, clarity, and a deeper connection to yourself and the world than you ever thought possible. You're being remade, from the inside out.

So yeah, you're exhausted. You're scared. You're probably wondering if you'll ever feel normal again. You won't. You'll feel something better. You'll feel more real. You'll feel more alive. But to get there, you have to walk through the fire. Don't bypass it. Don't pretend it's not happening. Lean into it. Feel it. Let it transform you. You've got this. Even when it feels like you don't. I promise you, there's light at the end of this very, very dark tunnel.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
